justification: All 7 rules identified with correct invariant classification. Refund window correctly marked invariant: false. Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1. Multiple ambiguities surfaced (timer start point, delivery definition, discount precedence, per-order vs per-line-item exclusivity). 11 acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. Confidence level of 72 is appropriate given the stated assumptions and unresolved ambiguities.

observations.text: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications
Refund window correctly classified as invariant: false with strong justification about policy flexibility
Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction clearly captured in BR-1 with explicit testable assertion
Discount code and promotion mutual exclusivity correctly identified in BR-5
11 acceptance criteria far exceeds the 4 minimum, with good boundary coverage
Multiple ambiguities surfaced: timer start point, delivery date definition, discount precedence, per-order vs per-line-item exclusivity
Excellent edge cases including race conditions at reservation boundary and mid-checkout promotion expiry
Comprehensive stakeholder impact analysis covering 6 distinct groups
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations

BR-2 marks the 15-minute reservation timeout as invariant: false, but the rubric expects invariant: true for the reservation release rule. However, the agent's reasoning is nuanced — distinguishing the specific duration (policy) from the concept of a finite timeout (invariant) — which is defensible
Bounded context is singular ('Order Management & Checkout') rather than identifying separate 'cart and checkout lifecycle' and 'inventory management' contexts as expected
